download_details.error: trafilatura returned empty text (page may be JS-rendered or paywalled)

findings: Existing nuclear regulations were designed for large traditional reactors and must be adapted for SMRs, creating regulatory uncertainty and time-consuming processes that drive deployment risk.
Regulatory risk is a primary SMR deployment risk category, arising from uncertainties in regulatory approval and discrepancies in nuclear safety regulatory frameworks across countries.
Regulatory hurdles — including extended licensing timelines, lack of standardization, and absence of international regulatory harmonization — are major drivers of SMR deployment risk.
High upfront capital costs, operating costs, and difficulty attracting investment are cited as critical economic risk factors for SMR deployment.

Securing financing for first-of-a-kind (FOAK) SMR projects is particularly risky because investors are hesitant to back a relatively new technology; government support is often necessary to bridge the financing gap.
